title: Firm-Style Probability Drills
subtitle: Jane Street–style puzzles: symmetry, updating, and quick EV
intro: Trading firms like Jane Street, SIG, and Optiver do not ask you to recite formulas — they watch you reason under uncertainty. The patterns repeat: compute an expected value, exploit symmetry, update beliefs when new information arrives, and explain your answer out loud.

This capstone lesson collects original drills inspired by those interview styles (probability & markets guides, classic puzzle collections, and public practice sites). Nothing here is copied from proprietary question banks; the scenarios are written for Probability Playground.

Treat each question like a live interview: state assumptions, show arithmetic, and sanity-check extremes. If you can pass this lesson, you are ready to pair our interactive content with the external resources hub for depth reading.

primer.heading: Solve it like a live interview
body: Firms grade your **process**, not just the number. Three moves carry most interviews: **translate** the word problem into a model (what are the outcomes and their probabilities or payoffs?), **pick a tool** quickly, and **sanity-check** the answer before you commit.
Narrate the whole way. State assumptions, compute out loud, and test extremes — does a probability land in `[0,1]`? Does a symmetric setup give a symmetric answer? Does a limiting case look right?

visual.caption: The interview loop
items: **Translate:** name the outcomes and their probabilities (or payoffs).
**Choose a tool:** EV, conditioning, symmetry, waiting time, or estimation.

primer.heading: Match the phrase to the tool
body: Most drills map to one of a handful of tools. The speed skill is recognizing the **phrase** that signals each tool, so you reach for it without hesitation.
When two tools could work, pick the one with the **cleaner arithmetic** — usually the complement, symmetry, or linearity.

primer.heading: Expected value is your edge
body: A bet is worth taking only if its **expected value** is positive after costs. Multiply each payoff by its probability, sum, then subtract any entry fee.
Sanity-check the sign: a rare large win can outweigh frequent small losses, and vice-versa.

example.prompt: Roll a die: win `$6` on a six, lose `$1` otherwise. Is it worth playing?
example.result: Positive edge → play. The rare `$6` win beats the frequent `$1` loss.

visual.expression: EV = Σ xᵢ · pᵢ   (then subtract the entry fee)
visual.caption: Probability-weighted payoff, net of cost.

primer.heading: Update when you learn something
body: New information **shrinks the sample space** — recount inside the world consistent with what you were told. Two recurring flavors: **'at least one'** conditioning and **drawing without replacement**.
Without replacement, every draw changes the counts: after pulling one red from `2R, 1B`, the urn is `1R, 1B`, so the next red is `1/2`, not the original `2/3`.

example.prompt: Two fair coins; you're told at least one is Heads. P(both Heads)?
steps: Condition on 'at least one H' → space is `{HH, HT, TH}`.

visual.caption: Renormalize within the event you were told occurred.

primer.heading: Waiting times: divide by the probability
body: 'How many independent tries until the first success?' is a **geometric** waiting time, and its mean is simply **`1/p`**.
So the first heads takes `1/(1/2) = 2` flips on average, and the first six on a die takes `1/(1/6) = 6` rolls. Quick to state, quick to check.

example.prompt: Flip a fair coin until the first Heads. Expected number of flips?

visual.caption: Mean of a Geometric(p) waiting time.

primer.heading: Market-making intuition
body: A market maker quotes a **bid** (buy price) and an **ask** (sell price) around an estimate of **fair value**. You earn the **spread**, but you face **adverse selection** — informed traders pick you off on the wrong side.

Two rules to internalize: buying **below** fair value (or selling **above** it) is `+EV`; and the **less certain** you are about fair value, the **wider** you should quote to protect against being picked off.

keyTerms.definition: Your best estimate of what the instrument is truly worth — for a die roll, `3.5`.
Ask minus bid; the maker's gross compensation for providing liquidity.
The risk that whoever trades against you knows something you don't.

example.prompt: Fair value of a die roll is `3.5`. A trader sells to you at your bid of `3`. Good trade?
steps: You bought at `3`; fair value is `3.5`.

visual.title: Adverse selection
visual.text: Tight quotes while you are uncertain invite getting picked off on both sides. Quote width should scale with your uncertainty about fair value.

primerNarration.title: Solve it out loud
primerNarration.caption: Firms grade how you think, not just your final number. Translate the story into outcomes and probabilities, pick a tool, then sanity-check against bounds and symmetry before you commit.
bullets: Translate the words into a model
Pick a tool: EV, conditioning, symmetry, waiting time, estimate

primerNarration.title: Match the phrase to the tool
primerNarration.caption: Most drills map to a handful of tools. Expected value for payoff games, conditioning when you learn something new, symmetry for interchangeable outcomes, and a simple one over p for waiting times.

primerNarration.title: Find your edge
primerNarration.caption: A bet is worth it only if the probability-weighted payoff beats the cost. Winning six dollars one time in six while losing a dollar the other five still nets a small positive edge of about seventeen cents a roll.

primerNarration.title: Recount what's left
primerNarration.caption: New information shrinks the sample space, so recount inside the world you were told about. Told at least one of two coins is heads, the chance both are heads is one third, not one quarter.

primerNarration.title: One over the probability
primerNarration.caption: How many tries until the first success? On average, one divided by the success probability. The first heads takes two flips, and the first six on a die takes six rolls.

primerNarration.title: Quote around fair value
primerNarration.caption: Quote a bid and an ask around fair value. You earn the spread, but informed traders can pick you off, so when you are less certain about fair value, quote wider.

visual.expression: buy below fair  /  sell above fair  =  +EV

questions.question: Two fair coins are hidden. You are told at least one is Heads. What is the probability both are Heads?

explanations.A: 1/4 is P(both Heads) unconditionally, but we are conditioning on at least one Head.
explanations.B: Correct. Conditional sample space {HH, HT, TH} — only one of three equally likely outcomes has two Heads.
explanations.C: 1/2 ignores that HT and TH are also consistent with 'at least one Head'.
explanations.D: 2/3 would require two favorable outcomes out of three for double Heads, which is not the case.

remediation.question: A fair coin is flipped twice. Given the first flip is Heads, P(both Heads)?

explanations.A: That is unconditional P(HH).
explanations.B: Correct — given first H, second is H with probability 1/2.
explanations.C: 2/3 is the 'at least one H' conditioning, not 'first is H'.
explanations.D: First flip fixed as H does not force second H.

remediation.question: Two dice show at least one six. Approximate P(both sixes)?

explanations.A: 1/36 is unconditional P(6,6).
explanations.B: Correct — 11 outcomes have ≥1 six; only (6,6) has both.
explanations.C: 1/6 treats the second die as independent without conditioning.
explanations.D: Too high — double six is rare even conditional on ≥1 six.

questions.question: You roll a fair die; you win $6 if it shows 6, otherwise you lose $1. What is the expected profit per roll?

explanations.A: −$0.50 treats the game as net-negative, but the one-in-six $6 win (+1) outweighs the 5/6 × $1 loss (−5/6), leaving a positive 1/6.
explanations.B: $0 would be a fair game. Here EV = (1/6)(6) + (5/6)(−1) = 1 − 5/6 = 1/6 ≈ $0.17, a small positive edge.
explanations.D: $1 would be the EV only with a far larger win (e.g. $16 on a six); the $6 payout gives just 1/6.

remediation.question: Win $16 on a six, lose $2 otherwise. EV per roll?

explanations.A: −$2 is the loss on a non-six only; the 1/6 chance of the $16 win pulls the average up to +$1.
explanations.C: $2 double-counts the win; netting the five $2 losses against the $16 win gives (16 − 10)/6 = $1.
explanations.D: $4 ignores the 5/6 chance of a $2 loss, which drags the EV down to $1.

remediation.question: Win $10 on heads, lose $4 on tails (fair coin). EV?

explanations.A: Ignores the +10 win half the time.
explanations.B: Understates the positive half.
explanations.D: Too high for this structure.

questions.question: Fair die roll. Fair value is 3.5. You quote 3 at 4 with a $1 spread. A trader sells to you at 3. Did you likely buy above or below fair value?
options: Below — you got a good buy

explanations.A: Buying at 3 when fair is 3.5 means you paid less than fair — a good buy for you.
explanations.B: Correct. Your bid at 3 is below expected value 3.5, so buying there is +EV for the market maker.
explanations.C: 3 ≠ 3.5; you traded on the bid side below midpoint.
explanations.D: Fair value comparison is enough to judge the trade direction.

remediation.question: Fair value 10. You sell at your ask of 11. Relative to fair, you:

explanations.A: Selling above fair is good for the market maker.
explanations.B: Correct — ask above fair value is +EV sale.
explanations.D: This is about price vs fair, not inventory alone.

remediation.question: Fair value 50. Quote 49 at 51. Midpoint of your quote is:

explanations.A: That is the bid only.
explanations.B: Correct — (49+51)/2 = 50, matching fair value here.
explanations.C: That is the ask only.
explanations.D: Sum, not midpoint.

questions.question: Urn: 2 red, 1 blue. Draw one — red. You draw again without replacement. P(second is red)?

explanations.A: 1/3 is P(first red), not second after first red.
explanations.B: Correct. After one red removed, urn has 1 red, 1 blue → P(red) = 1/2.
explanations.C: 2/3 was P(first red); second draw has fewer reds left.
explanations.D: Second draw is not certain red.

remediation.question: An urn has 3 red, 2 blue. The first draw is red (no replacement). P(second red)?

explanations.A: Correct. After removing one red, 2 red and 2 blue remain, so P(second red) = 2/4 = 1/2.
explanations.B: 2/5 counts the 2 remaining reds but keeps the total at 5 — it forgets the drawn red also leaves the denominator.
explanations.C: 3/5 is the original P(first red); it ignores the update after a red is removed.
explanations.D: 3/4 overstates it: only 2 reds remain among 4 balls, not 3 of 4.

remediation.question: Two cards from a deck without replacement. First is an ace. P(second is ace)?

explanations.A: 1/13 is single-draw ace probability.
explanations.B: Correct — 3 aces remain in 51 cards.
explanations.C: 4/52 ignores the first ace drawn.
explanations.D: 1/4 treats draws as independent.

questions.question: A and B each independently pick 0 or 1 uniformly at random (no communication or shared strategy). You see the sum mod 2. If it is 0, the team wins $3. Entry fee is $1. Should the team play?

explanations.A: Even with independent random picks the sum is even half the time (both 0 or both 1), so the team wins with prob 1/2 — positive EV.
explanations.B: Correct. Win $3 with prob 1/2, pay $1 → EV = 1.5 − 1 = 0.5.
explanations.C: Overstates EV — the team wins only half the time.
explanations.D: Far too high.

remediation.question: Same game but win $5 with prob 1/2, fee $1. EV?

explanations.A: $1 understates the win: 0.5($5) − $1 = $1.50, not $1.
explanations.C: $2 would need a higher payout or win probability; the arithmetic gives $1.50.
explanations.D: $2.50 is the gross expected win 0.5($5) before subtracting the $1 fee.

remediation.question: Win $3 with prob 1/3, fee $1. EV?

explanations.A: −$1 would be the result if you won nothing; the (1/3)($3) expected win exactly cancels the fee.
explanations.C: $0.33 looks like (1/3) of something but forgets the $3 payout cancels the whole $1 fee.
explanations.D: $1 is the gross expected win before the fee; net EV is $0.

questions.question: You are very uncertain about fair value on a novelty dice game. Should your bid-ask spread be wider or narrower than when you are confident?

explanations.A: Correct. More uncertainty → wider spread to protect against adverse selection.
explanations.B: Tight spreads when uncertain invite getting picked off on both sides.
explanations.C: Good market makers adjust width to confidence.
explanations.D: Both sides typically widen symmetrically around fair value.

remediation.question: New public info sharply reduces your uncertainty. Spread should:

explanations.A: Less uncertainty usually allows tighter quotes.
explanations.B: Correct — you can compete more aggressively when risk is lower.
explanations.C: Quotes should reflect updated beliefs.
explanations.D: You still need a spread for inventory risk.

remediation.question: Main reason market makers widen spreads:

explanations.A: Not the primary economic reason.
explanations.B: Correct — compensation for risk and adverse selection.
explanations.C: Spreads are economic, not theatrical.
explanations.D: Quotes are intentional.

questions.question: Repeated fair coin flips until first Heads. Expected number of flips?

explanations.A: 1 assumes the first flip is always Heads; on average you need 1/p = 2 flips.
explanations.B: 1.5 is a guess; the Geometric(1/2) mean is 1/p = 2, not 1.5.
explanations.C: Correct — expected waiting time for success probability 1/2 is 1/p = 2 trials.
explanations.D: 4 = 1/p with p = 1/4; for a fair coin p = 1/2, giving 2.

remediation.question: Flips until six on a fair die. Expected rolls?

explanations.A: 3 undershoots; the mean is 1/(1/6) = 6.
explanations.B: 4 is still below 1/p = 6 — the waiting time for a 1/6 event is longer.
explanations.C: Correct — mean of Geometric(1/6) is 1/p = 6 rolls.
explanations.D: 36 = 6² confuses the mean with a variance-style scale; the mean is 6.

remediation.question: Success prob 1/3 each trial. Expected trials until success?

explanations.A: 1/3 is the success probability p itself, not the mean 1/p.
explanations.B: 1 would be the mean only if success were certain each trial.
explanations.C: Correct — mean = 1/p = 1/(1/3) = 3 trials.
explanations.D: 9 = 1/p² is a variance-style scale, not the mean 1/p = 3.

questions.question: From a standard deck, two cards drawn without replacement. P(both hearts)?

explanations.A: 1/16 = (13/52)² treats the draws as independent; without replacement the second draw has only 12 hearts in 51 cards.
explanations.C: 25/1024 is not a valid reduction here; the exact dependent product is (13/52)(12/51) = 1/17.
explanations.D: 1/4 is P(one card is a heart), not the probability that both are.

remediation.question: P(both aces from two cards)?

explanations.A: 1/169 = (1/13)² treats the draws as independent; without replacement use (4/52)(3/51).
explanations.C: 1/13 is the chance one card is an ace, not both.
explanations.D: 2/52 adds the ace counts instead of multiplying the two dependent draws.

remediation.question: One heart drawn. P(second is heart)?

explanations.A: Correct — one heart is gone, leaving 12 hearts in 51 cards.
explanations.B: 13/52 ignores that the first heart was removed from both counts.
explanations.C: 1/4 = 13/52 is the unconditional heart probability, before any draw.
explanations.D: 12/52 updates the numerator but forgets the deck now holds only 51 cards.

questions.question: In a quant interview, which habit helps most when stuck?
options: Stay silent until you have the exact answer
Talk through assumptions and try small cases
Ask to use a calculator immediately
Change the problem to an easier one without telling the interviewer

explanations.A: Silence hides your reasoning — interviewers grade the process.
explanations.B: Correct. Narrating assumptions and testing small cases is exactly what firms like Jane Street encourage.
explanations.C: Mental math matters; calculators are rarely the first move.
explanations.D: Never silently change the problem — clarify with the interviewer instead.

remediation.question: Best first step on an unfamiliar puzzle:

explanations.A: Guessing wastes the collaborative format.
explanations.B: Correct — structure before computation.
explanations.C: Formulas come after understanding.
explanations.D: Never skip without engaging.

remediation.question: Interviewer gives a hint. You should:

explanations.A: Hints are gifts — use them.
explanations.B: Correct — shows adaptability.
explanations.C: They want your reasoning, not theirs read aloud.
explanations.D: Explain the pivot out loud.

questions.question: Order-of-magnitude: about how many fair coin flips give an expected count of 50 Heads?

explanations.A: 10 flips → only 5 expected heads.
explanations.B: 50 flips → 25 expected heads.
explanations.C: Correct — expected heads = n/2, so n = 100 for 50 heads.
explanations.D: 1000 flips → 500 expected heads.

remediation.question: Fair coin: expected Heads in 80 flips?

explanations.A: 20 is a quarter of the flips; expected heads is half, since p = 1/2.
explanations.C: 80 would require every flip to land heads with certainty.
explanations.D: 160 doubles the flip count; expected heads is 80 × 1/2 = 40.

remediation.question: Quick sanity: 1000 flips, expected Heads near:

explanations.A: 100 is a tenth of the flips; expected heads is half of 1000.
explanations.B: 250 is a quarter of the flips, not half.
explanations.D: 900 would require almost every flip to land heads, far above the 1/2 rate.

explanations.A: Heads is possible.
explanations.B: 1/4 is not fair coin.
explanations.C: Correct.
explanations.D: Not certain.

placementQuestions.question: Expected value of fair die roll?

explanations.A: Slightly low.
explanations.C: High.
explanations.D: Maximum face, not mean.

placementQuestions.question: At least one head in two fair flips — how many outcomes in sample space?

explanations.A: Too few.
explanations.B: That counts favorable, not total space.
explanations.C: Correct — HH, HT, TH, TT.
explanations.D: Three flips.

explanations.A: Half the gap.
explanations.B: Correct — 10 − 8.
explanations.C: Bid size.
explanations.D: Ask size.

explanations.A: Positive EV exists.
explanations.B: Too low.
explanations.C: Correct — 0.5 × 4.
explanations.D: Full win not guaranteed.

explanations.A: Correct — (1/2)².
explanations.B: One head prob.
explanations.C: At least one head.
explanations.D: Not certain.

explanations.A: Correct.
explanations.B: Opposite.
explanations.C: Never zero in practice.
explanations.D: Spread reflects uncertainty.

placementQuestions.question: Geometric with p=1/2, mean trials until success?

explanations.A: Too low.
explanations.B: Correct — 1/p.
explanations.C: Higher p needed.
explanations.D: Too high.
